title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
audience: ML students who know supervised fine-tuning and preference pairs, but are new to RLHF objectives.
central_question: How can a language model learn directly from chosen-vs-rejected responses without training a separate reward model?

teaching_units.name: Preference Pair Input
teaching_units.goal: Introduce prompts, chosen responses, rejected responses, and the role of human or AI preference labels.
formulas: (x, y_w, y_l) where y_w is preferred over y_l
teaching_units.visual_example: A prompt card branches to a green chosen answer and a red rejected answer, with the preference label between them.

teaching_units.name: Reference Policy Anchor
teaching_units.goal: Show that DPO compares the current policy preference gap against a fixed reference policy gap.
teaching_units.visual_example: Two side-by-side bar charts compare winner-minus-loser gaps for the trainable policy and reference policy.

teaching_units.name: DPO Margin Loss
teaching_units.goal: Explain how the margin turns into a logistic loss that pushes the policy toward the preferred answer.
teaching_units.visual_example: A margin slider moves right; the loss curve drops as the chosen response becomes more favored than the rejected one.

teaching_units.name: DPO vs Reward-Model RLHF
teaching_units.goal: Contrast the usual reward-model-plus-RL route with direct preference optimization.
formulas: RLHF: preferences -> reward model -> RL update
teaching_units.visual_example: A two-lane pipeline shows the reward-model detour on top and the shorter DPO route below.

comparison_axes: reward-model RLHF vs direct preference optimization
policy preference gap vs reference preference gap
formula correctness vs visual grounding of chosen/rejected responses
